questionText: ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) You went to a Fuel Service Station to buy a litre of kerosene but you were informed that the stock available was contaminated with some benzoic acid. You need the kerosene to perform an experiment so you are forced to buy the impure one with the intention of purifying it. You cannot do a distillation but you can purify it by another method of your choice. How would you purify the kerosene? Provide a list of all materials including chemicals needed for the purification. (C) Solution Apparatus/Chemicals Separating funnel, conical or cylindrical Glass funnel Beaker Conical flask Glass rod Filter paper NaOH or KOH solution Anhydrous Na2SO4 or MgSO4 Procedure Transfer the impure kerosene into a separating funnel using a glass/simple funnel. Add about 100 cm3 of NaOH or KOH solution to the kerosene. Stopper the funnel and shake vigorously. Drain out the aqueous layer and add fresh NaOH or KOH solution. Shake and drain the aqueous layer. Wash the kerosene about three times, each time with about 50 cm3 of water. Drain the kerosene into a conical flask. Add anhydrous Na2SO4 or MgSO4 until the solid can move freely when the flask is swirled around. Filter the kerosene into a suitable, clean container through a fluted filter paper and stopper the container. ROUND THREE (True or False) Q. Adjacent angles on a straight line are supplementary. (M)

questionText: ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) (C) You have been provided all the apparatus needed to prepare a column for column chromatograph to separate two coloured compounds. Describe how the column will be set up. Solution Procedure The glass column is washed thoroughly with detergent, rinsed and dried in the oven at 110-120°C. The column is clamped upright, on a retort stand. A small plug of cotton or glass wool is pushed down to the bottom of the column. The column is filled with petroleum ether (boiling range 60-80°C). A suitable quantity of the adsorbent is weighed in a beaker. A small volume of petroleum ether is added and the mixture stirred to give a slurry. Using a funnel, and while stirring, the slurry is added, small quantities at a time to the column whose tap is left open. The column is gently tapped with the glass rod to prevent any air bubble being formed or trapped. When all the slurry has been added, there should be some solvent on top of the adsorbent. questionText: ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) (B) Study specimens A and B carefully and answer the following questions. Identify fully specimens A and B. Give one obvious reason each to support your
correctAnswer: State three observable adaptable features in specimen A for feeding by the animal. State three observable adaptive features in specimen B for feeding by the animal. Solution Specimen A - Lower jaw bone of a herbivore Specimen B - Lower jaw bone of carnivore Reasons: Specimen A - Presence of Diastema Specimen B - Presence of well-developed canine teeth (carnassial teeth) Three observable adaptable features of specimen A to its diet Sharp (Chisel-like) incisors for cutting vegetation (Leaves/food) Ridged surface of molars and premolars for grinding/chewing food. Diastema provides space for the tongue to turn food during chewing. (manipulation of food bolus keeps chewed grass separate from fresh grass) Three observable adaptable features of specimen B to its diet The incisor teeth are chisel shaped for removing scraps of meat from bones (Nibbling/gripping) Prominent curved and pointed canine teeth to pierce the prey and hold it firmly. Molars and premolars have sharp and pointed surfaces for crushing bones and cutting the meat into smaller pieces

subject: Chemistry
questionText: ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) (C) You are given a white, powdered inorganic solid, which is supposed to be an intimate mixture of Na2CO3 and NaHCO3 but the proportions are not known. The task is to determine these proportions by a volumetric analysis. List all the reagents, glassware and other apparatus needed for the analysis Describe how you would proceed to do the analysis Write the chemical equations for the reactions that take place Indicate the volumes/titres that will be used in calculating the relative proportions. Solution Apparatus and Reagents Weighing bottle Spatula 250 cm3 Volumetric flask 250 cm3 Conical flasks Funnel Beaker, 50 or 100 cm3 Pipette 10 or 20 or 25 cm3 Burette 20 or 25 cm3 Standardised HCl solution approximately 0.10 moldm-3 Indicators, phenolphthalein and methyl orange De-ionised or distilled water Procedure Weigh a calculated mass of the solid using the weighing bottle (w1) Transfer the weighed solid to a beaker and weigh the bottle again (w2) Dissolve the solid in de-ionised or distilled water Transfer the solution into the volumetric flask using the funnel Make the solution up to the mark with more water Shake the flask to obtain a homogeneous solution Pipette 10 or 20 or 25 cm3 of the solution and titrate it against the standardised HCl solution using phenolphthalein as indicator Repeat until at least 2 consistent titres are obtained; average titre V1 Repeat the titration using methyl orange as indicator, average titre V2 Chemical equations With phenolphthalein With methyl orange Volumes to be used: ROUND THREE (True or False) Q.
